top_comment.author: Matthew_Opitz
top_comment.text:
This sort of "meta-strategy" would be far more effective if we knew exactly where the red button was (where the level was when AGI would reach a point of truly dangerous, out-of-our-control capability).  In that scenario where we had perfect knowledge of where the red button was, the counter-intuitively perfect strategy would be to open-source everything and allow for, or positively invite, every sort of potential harmful use of AGI right up until that point.  We would have many (hopefully minuscule) AI-Chernobyls, many empirical examples on a smaller scale of instrumental convergence, mesa-optimizing, out-of-distribution behavior, etc.  Probably enough examples even for mainstream laypeople to grok these concepts.  

Then, under this ideal scenario, society would collectively turn-on-a-dime and employ every lesson we learned from the previous reckless epoch to making AI provably, ironclad-ly aligned before taking even a single additional step forward.  

The obstacles to employing this ideal meta-strategy are:

 1. Not knowing exactly where the red button is (i.e. the level at which AGI would forever slip out of our control). 
 2. Not having the coordination needed among humans to stop on a dime once we are closely approaching that level in order to thoroughly shift our object-level strategy in line with our overall meta-strategy (which is, to be clear, to have an object-level-strategy of recklessness up until we approach AGI escape, and then shift to an opposite object-level-strategy of extreme caution from that point onwards).
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: This sort of "meta-strategy" would be far more effective if we knew exactly where the red button was (where the level was when AGI would reach a point of truly dangerous, out-of-our-control capability).

summary: The main claim is: However, this information in repeated plays is shared between the agent and the one who pretends to be a super-predictor.
This means, if I pretend to be a super-predictor, I can tell whether the agent tries to "break out" by checking whether some output results in unexpected two-boxing on Newcomb-like benchmarks.
The main claim is: That suggests the idea that the "operator" of an LLM should be another, smaller AI wrapped around it, trained for alignment.

For the moment, handwave it with "the wrapper is smaller, and its interactions with the LLM are text-based, so training it for alignment should be simpler than training a big opaque AI for both intelligence and alignment at once".
The main claim is: Therefore, it would likely be placed closer to the "precision" end of the continuum, perhaps around a value of 6 or 7.
When you place “trustworfh” on that continuum, what value does it have?

top_comment.author: MSRayne
top_comment.text: It makes a lot of obvious errors. Cricket is obviously closer to eagle than to stove because they're both living things, for instance. And calling kamikaze cowardly! That's utterly absurd. You can tell towards the end it's just randomly placing things while confabulating false explanations.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: Cricket is obviously closer to eagle than to stove because they're both living things, for instance.
